问.wiki_summary: Phono-semantic compound: semantic 口 + phonetic 門 (OC *mɯːn); an exoactive derivation of 聞 meaning 'let (me) hear'.

很.wiki_summary: Phono-semantic compound: semantic 彳 (step/walk) + phonetic 艮 (OC *kɯːns); original meaning 'cruel/to defy' shifted to intensifier 'very' since the Yuan dynasty.

最.wiki_summary: Phono-semantic compound: originally 㝡, semantic 冃 (roof/cap) + phonetic 取; later modified with distinguishing marks to mean 'the most'.
最.difference: Our data classifies 最 as a compound ideograph (會意), but Wiktionary identifies it as a phono-semantic compound with 取 as phonetic.

重.wiki_summary: Phono-semantic compound and ideogrammic compound: semantic 人 (man) + phonetic 東 (bag) + 土 (earth), depicting a man carrying a heavy bag standing on ground.
重.difference: Wiktionary lists both phono-semantic and ideogrammic compound labels (in that order); our data lists only compound_ideograph, which is the secondary label.

并.wiki_summary: From the clerical script of 竝; originally depicted two people standing side-by-side, meaning 'side by side' or 'together'.

物.wiki_summary: Phono-semantic compound: semantic 牛 (cattle) + phonetic 勿; cognate with Proto-Sino-Tibetan roots meaning 'grain/seed', broadly meaning 'things/matter'.

应.wiki_summary: Phono-semantic compound: semantic 心 (heart) + phonetic 䧹 (OC *qɯŋ); etymologically related to 膺, meaning 'should/ought to/respond'.

战.wiki_summary: Phono-semantic compound: semantic 戈 (halberd) + phonetic 單 (OC *taːn); literally 'battling with a halberd', with possible Proto-Sino-Tibetan roots meaning 'tremble'.

头.wiki_summary: Phono-semantic compound: semantic 頁 (head) + phonetic 豆 (OC *doːs); meaning 'head', with possible Proto-Sino-Tibetan cognates.

文.wiki_summary: Ideogram (指事): depicts a man with a painted or tattooed chest; original meaning 'tattoo/pattern' evolved to 'written character', 'literature', and 'refined/cultured'.
文.difference: Our data classifies 文 as a pictograph (象形), but Wiktionary labels it an Ideogram (指事/simple indicative), depicting the chest marking as an added indicative element rather than a pure pictorial representation.
